title: Evangelion and the classic Drama
wikitext:
[]: Evangelion and the classic Drama             Quote Post by Kendrix » Wed Dec 22, 2010 7:46 am This is just something I've noticed, since they made us do the whole Five-Act-Classical-Drama thing to Death in school. One of the main differences to contemporal stories was that most (or most I knew at this point) don't have their big climax at the middle, but at the end. I wondered wether I actually knew something that had 'falling action'. (i.e, everything slowly breaking down after a big showdown.) I realized that I did: Evangelion. I don't think it was intentional, but it's internal structure does have some semblance to that of a classical drama à la Aristoteles (tough I'm not sure if 'classical drama' is the correct english word : Act I: EXPOSITION We get to know the protagonists, their world and their conflicts This are either eps 1 and 2 or, more likely and fitting the structure of the classic drama better, eps 1 to 7: Boy with Daddy issues is supposed to get into mech and save humanity. Boy DOES NOT WANT. Boy gets convinced via power of boobs. Boy stays in the fortress city, but doesn't know wheter he really wants to get into mech or why We also learn that they live in a dark, post or pre? apocalyptic world with lots of dark secrets. Beginning of Boy's relationship with Chick a). Chick a) likes Boy's Daddy a lot - maybe he's not evil? Act II: RISING ACTION More suspense is generated, conflicts deepen, increasingly cool stuff happens. In EVA, that would be eps 8-13 or 8-16 depending on interpretation: Angel war rages on, chick B arrives --> luv triangle, kids have to learn team work and increasingly kick ass. Boy and Chick a) start comming out of their shells, Chick B seems to be learning how to live with Boy. Boy's Daddy shows trust in him (ep. 11) and seems to begin to appreaciate his efforts ( ep. 12) It looks as if there's still hope. Act III: CLIMAX Conflicts come to full bloom, everything comes to a peak, there's a big, action loaded turning point. In EVA, that's either just the 'fourth Child trilogy' (17-19) or the entirety of 14-19. Everything's comming to a peak, everyone's relationship reaches a maximum: Even the Beta couples (Misato/Kaji, Touji/Hikari). Boy gets cute scenes with both his luv intrest whom he seems to have come closer to. (Asuka: Kiss, her visiting him at the hospital; Rei: Getting angry at Asuka in ep 16, "Thank you" usw), Boy even manages to get closer to Daddy --> Conversation at the grave. Then: Big BOOM. Bardiel incident. Daddy gives Boy a chance to fight, but Boy won't, showing some noble streak in the wrong place. Daddy saves Boy's life - By using an unstable control system that ends up maiming the protagonist's BBF. Big turning point. Boy is PISSED AS HELL, his relationship with his Daddy shattered beyond repair: Gone is all the indecisiveness. Shin-chan DOES NOT WANT and leaves mech behing once and for all. As he sits in his EMO corner, world is in Danger and chicks lose despite their best efforts. Boy is told abut impending end of the world an realizes that he HIMSELF has a wish to get into mech. Boy jumps into mech and goes BALISTIC, acting like a man again, but reality is a bitch and battery runs out. Boy goes "Mommy help me!" Scary stuff Boy had no control of happens. This is the direction the rest of the story will take from now on. Act IV: FALLING ACTION Everything goes to hell, piece by piece. Might contain minor hope spot/little climax These are eps 20 to 24. The 'minor Climax/hope spot' is ep 24 where Shinji briefly seems to have met someone to give him luv and cheer him up, but it still ends badly. The 'everything going to hell' aspect needs no explanation, I guess. Chick A dead and replaced by indentical loking clone, chick B broken beyond help, Mentor Figure 1 dead, mentor Figure 2 falling appart /depressed over loved one's death. No real interaction between Boy and Poppy anymore, friends move away... And protagonist feels powerless to stop it. Act V: Catastrophy/Solution. EoE or EoTV. Or both, if you chose to interpret them as the same. It is arguably both a catastrophy AND a Solution... Everyone Dies, Poppy luvd boy all along but boy doesn't find out, boy at the lowest of the low... And he STILL choses to live. WOW.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Dec 22, 2010 7:58 am Back when I first got into NGE, I wrote about this structure quite a bit, but nobody every seemed interested. FWIW, I do agree that NGE fits the classic 5-act structure pretty well, even though it also works well if you split it into two halves (first half being set-up, second half being deconstruction). Thankfully someone's finally decided to devote a thread to it, though.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Wed Dec 22, 2010 8:17 am Eva Yojimbo wrote: Back when I first got into NGE, I wrote about this structure quite a bit, but nobody every seemed interested. FWIW, I do agree that NGE fits the classic 5-act structure pretty well, even though it also works well if you split it into two halves (first half being set-up, second half being deconstruction). Thankfully someone's finally decided to devote a thread to it, though.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Dec 22, 2010 8:35 am Kendrix wrote: So I'm not the only one who notived?

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Wed Dec 22, 2010 10:03 am Eva Yojimbo wrote: We're all standing on the shoulders of giants; I doubt I was the first to notice either. The 5-act structure would be known to anyone who studied drama. Not ALL, but I still think the second half has a distinctly different feeling, tone, style, and focus than the first. Most of the deeper elements in the first half are under the surface (one reason I don't think it gets enough credit for being as good and important as it is) while the plot, action, and comedy are more on the surface. The second half largely inverts that paradigm. The thematic focus takes on an expositional quality.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Dec 22, 2010 10:34 am Kendrix wrote: if you look at the plot density alone, it's pretty even.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Wed Dec 22, 2010 11:12 am Eva Yojimbo wrote: Hmmm? I think it's very NOT even if we're talking about comparing the first half to the second half, but I think it's that way by design.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Dec 22, 2010 10:09 pm ^ Aha, OK, I getcha now. "Equal with less", in other words.
